name: Tane
description: Tāne is a major god in Māori mythology, representing the origin of forests and birds. He is the son of Ranginui (the sky father) and Papatūānuku (the earth mother), who were locked in a tight embrace, preventing light and life from entering the world. Tāne, along with his brothers, grew weary of living in darkness. He attempted to separate his parents, eventually succeeding by pushing Ranginui upwards with his legs, creating the space between the sky and the earth and allowing light to flood in. This act brought life into the world and established Tāne as a vital creator figure. Tāne is also credited with creating the first human, Hineahuone, from earth. He is associated with all living things in the forest, including trees, birds, and insects. His influence extends to the creation of canoes and other wooden objects, highlighting his role as a provider and innovator. Tāne is a symbol of strength, perseverance, and the power to overcome obstacles, playing a crucial role in the Māori creation narrative and the natural world.
subtitle: God of Forests, Birds, and Light

keyMyths.title: Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Tāne pushes his parents, Ranginui and Papatūānuku, apart to bring light and life into the world. This act creates the space between the sky and the earth, ending the darkness.

keyMyths.title: Creation of Hineahuone
keyMyths.description: Tāne creates the first woman, Hineahuone, from earth, who becomes the mother of humankind. This act signifies Tāne's role in the propagation of life.
keyMyths.source: Te Ara - the Encyclopedia of New Zealand

keyMyths.title: Tāne's Ascent to the Sky
keyMyths.description: Tāne climbs to the highest heaven to retrieve the baskets of knowledge (kete wananga) and bring them back to humanity. This shows Tāne as a bringer of wisdom and learning.

sources.author: Sir George Grey
sources.description: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.
sources.text: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.

sources.author: A.W. Reed
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Māori mythology.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Māori mythology.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Goddess of Magic & Motherhood | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of the Sky | Egyptian Mythology
Goddess of Love & Joy | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Greenery
worshipPractices.description: Māori traditionally offered branches, leaves, and other greenery to Tāne as a sign of respect and to ensure the continued health and abundance of the forests. These offerings were often placed at the base of trees or in sacred groves.

worshipPractices.name: Ritual Carvings
worshipPractices.description: Carved representations of Tāne, often incorporated into meeting houses (wharenui) and other important structures, served as focal points for reverence and were believed to embody his protective presence. The carvings would often depict themes of creation and the separation of Ranginui and Papatūānuku.

festivals.name: Matariki
festivals.description: While not exclusively dedicated to Tāne, the Matariki celebration, marking the Māori New Year, involves acknowledging the bounty of the land and forests, domains associated with Tāne. It is a time for planting and ensuring a fruitful harvest, connecting to Tāne's role as a provider.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tāne's legacy persists in contemporary Māori art, conservation efforts, and cultural identity. He symbolizes the interconnectedness of nature and humanity, inspiring environmental stewardship and a deep respect for the natural world. His story continues to be shared, reinforcing the importance of balance and harmony.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In the primordial darkness, Ranginui (the sky father) and Papatūānuku (the earth mother) were locked in an eternal embrace, their offspring trapped in the cramped space between them. Tāne was one of these children, yearning for light and space. The brothers debated how to separate their parents, some suggesting violence. Tāne proposed a gentler approach: pushing his parents apart. After many attempts by his siblings failed, Tāne, with his immense strength and determination, placed his shoulders against Papatūānuku and his feet against Ranginui. With a mighty heave, he forced them apart, creating the world of light and space that we know today. This act, while liberating for humanity, caused great sorrow to Ranginui and Papatūānuku, whose tears are said to be the rain and mist we experience.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: Beyond the separation of his parents, Tāne is known for several other key deeds. He adorned his father, Ranginui, with stars, bringing beauty to the heavens. He also sought to bring life to the world, creating the first woman, Hineahuone, from the earth. Tāne carefully sculpted her form and breathed life into her nostrils, marking the beginning of humanity. Another significant myth involves Tāne's quest to obtain the baskets of knowledge (ngā kete o te wānanga) from the heavens, guarded by his brother, Tāwhirimātea, the god of storms. This quest represents the pursuit of wisdom and understanding, highlighting the importance of knowledge in Māori culture. These stories illustrate Tāne's role as a creator, provider, and a champion of humanity.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: While there were no formal temples dedicated solely to Tāne, reverence for him was deeply embedded in daily life. Māori communities showed respect through sustainable practices, ensuring the forests remained healthy and abundant. Karakia (prayers or incantations) were often recited to acknowledge Tāne's presence and seek his blessings for successful hunts, harvests, and journeys through the forest. Specific trees, especially large and ancient ones, were considered sacred and treated with utmost respect. The act of carving wood, a vital skill for creating tools, canoes, and buildings, was also seen as a connection to Tāne. By using the resources he provided, Māori acknowledged their dependence on him and sought to maintain a harmonious relationship with the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Tāne's influence extends far beyond ancient myths. He remains a potent symbol of environmentalism and the interconnectedness of all living things. In contemporary Māori society, his story is used to promote conservation efforts and sustainable resource management. Artists and writers continue to draw inspiration from his tales, reinterpreting them for modern audiences. The concept of kaitiakitanga (guardianship), emphasizing the responsibility to care for the environment, is directly linked to Tāne's role as the protector of the forests. He serves as a reminder of the importance of balance, respect, and the enduring power of nature. His name and image are frequently used in environmental campaigns and educational programs, ensuring his legacy continues to inspire future generations.

powers: Creation of forests and plant life
Control over birds and forest creatures
shortDescription: Tāne is a principal god in Māori mythology, the creator of forests and birds, and the separator of Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother).
